text: You know, in the course of this series,
I've covered projects derailed by all
manner of disasters, mishaps, clashing
egos, studio interference, and sheer bad
luck. But I don't think I've ever
encountered a movie where God himself
tried to sabotage the thing. Such is the
case though with The Exorcist, William
Friedkin's infamous 1973 horror flick
about a pair of priests battling to free
a little girl from demonic possession.
It's actually kind of funny looking back
on the movie now cuz I suspect that
modern Hollywood would side with the
demon.
While the film itself is a tightly wound
nightmare about faith, doubt,
redemption, and little girls that look
like they just visited the world's worst
chiropractor, the real horror was going
on behind the camera. And believe me
when I say this particular cluster [ __ ]
was one of biblical proportions.
injuries and deaths, food poisoning,
malfunctioning equipment, budget
overruns, fires that destroyed entire
sets. The Exorcist was practically a
greatest hits tour for every movie based
[ __ ] that it's possible to have to the
point where cast and crew genuinely
began to believe that the shoot was
cursed and even had to bring an actual
priest in for protection. Never has the
term production hell been more
appropriate than right here. So let us
learn about the story of the exorcist
together, shall we? The story begins in
1971 with the release of Peter Blatty's
novel of the same name, which was
loosely based on a real life exorcism
case from the 1940s. The book struggled
to sell until Blatty's controversial
appearance on the Dick Cavet show
electrified audiences and pretty soon
the novel was a bestseller and Warner
Brothers had picked up the film rights
with Blatty managing to negotiate
himself into screenwriting duties.
William Friedkin, fresh off the success
of the French Connection, was attached
to direct with a modest budget of 5
million allocated for the movie.
Casting, however, proved to be tricky.
The studio wanted big names like Marlon
Brandle, Audrey Hetburn, Jack Nicholson,
and even Paul Newman. But Frein was
adamant. He wanted actors, not stars,
and he got them. Max Vonido, a Swedish
actor who was only 43 years old at the
time, was cast as the elderly father
Marin and aged up with some of the best
prosthetics that I've ever seen. For
most of my life, I honestly believed
that guy had just been [ __ ] old for
the past 50 years. The role of the
younger father Carus initially went to
veteran character actor Stacy Keech
until Fredin met with stage actor Jason
Miller. Miller himself was Catholic and
had even studied to be a priest before
having a spiritual crisis much like his
character. After reading the novel, he
flat out told Friedkin, "Caras is me."
Pretty soon, he was in and Cech was out.
Reagan was a tougher nut to crack,
though. The film needed a young actress
who looked innocent enough to be
sympathetic, but who could also pull off
some pretty dark and mature scenes. A
bunch of potential candidates were
tested out, but none of them really fit
the bill. Pamelan Ferdinan was rejected
for being too well-known at the time.
Denise Nickerson was interested but
found the material too dark and soon
backed out. Even Jaime Lee Curtis was
approached but her mom Janet Lei took
one look at the script and said, "Fuck
that. No way." Frekin was on the verge
of conceding defeat and cast in an older
actress when out of the blue an unknown
12-year-old named Linda Blair was
brought into his office and immediately
impressed him with her mature take on
the material. Our screen test with Ellen
Buren. Her on-screen mother went so well
that Frein declared, "There wasn't one
other actress I would have considered."
With cast and crew finally ready, it was
time to get to work. The budget, $5
million. The schedule, 85 days of
shooting. Neither of those things would
turn out to be true. First came the
fire. The massive three-story McNeel
house set built inside a New York
warehouse burned to the [ __ ] ground
in the middle of the night. The cause? A
pigeon flew into a circuit box, shorted
the whole thing out, and started an
electrical fire that engulfed the
building.
Really,
>> a pigeon did all of that. Not an
electrical fault, not arson, not a
disgruntled teamster, a bird.
Apparently, the only set that survived
on damage was Reagan's bedroom, the very
room where the demon does his best work.
Coincidence? Uh, but okay, fine.
Accidents happen. The set was rebuilt,
forcing a six-w weekek delay, and a
brand new sprinkler system was installed
just in case. A sprinkler system that
immediately malfunctioned and flooded
the entire place, forcing another twoe
delay.
Damn, talk about bad luck, eh? That
wasn't the only technical mishap,
either. Some of the exorcism scenes
required sudden temperature drops on
set, meaning high-powered air cooler
units had to be brought in.
Unfortunately, they also malfunctioned
during filming and instead of cold air,
they started blasting actual snow onto
the set. It wasn't much fun for the
actors either. Linda Blair ended up with
a lifelong fear of the cold after
spending weeks shooting scenes in
sub-zero temperatures wearing just a
night gown. Logistics also turned out to
be a bit of a problem. The scenes meant
to be shot in Iraq were delayed when
half the crew were struck down with
dissentry and sunstroke and the statue
of Pizuzu got lost in transit and ended
up in Hong Kong instead of Iraq, forcing
two weeks of delays. Fredin himself
proved to be a difficult director to
work with. On the very first day of
shooting, he had a wall removed to
create space for a dolly to back up from
a shot of frying bacon, but he became
fixated on the way the meat curled up
while it was cooking and couldn't seem
to get it just right. One crew member
who was off sick for three days came
back to find him still working on the
same scene. It wasn't exactly [ __ ]
party time for the actors either. He
liked to get genuine reactions from his
performers, so he'd do neat little
tricks like firing off blank gunshots at
random times to keep them scared, or
having fake vomit projected right into
Jason Miller's face without warning him,
or even straight up slapping a real
priest just before the cameras rolled to
shake him up. It wasn't just freaking
making life miserable for the actors,
either. A scene where Ellen Buren gets
yanked backward by a stunt harness and
thrown against a wall went badly wrong
and jerked her so hard that it fractured
her cockix. And they even decided to
keep that take in the film because it
was so realistic.
She had to be on crutches for the rest
of the shoot and the injury left her in
permanent pain for years afterwards.
Linda Blair suffered a fractured spine
because she wasn't properly strapped
into the bed harness while the thing was
shaking like a [ __ ] Bronco. again.
She was basically forced to work through
the injury and was left in chronic pain
for the rest of her life. A carpenter
sliced his own thumb off and another
technician cut his toe off. This was all
bad enough, but then the dying began.
First to go was Linda Blair's
grandfather who died in the first week
of production. Tragic, I know, but
that's what old people tend to do, so I
guess you could overlook that at least.
On the other hand, Max Vonido's brother
passed away suddenly on his very first
day of shooting, forcing a delay while
he traveled back to Sweden to take care
of the funeral. The actor Jack McGawan
contracted the flu and died shortly
after completing his scenes. The actress
playing Caris's mother also died midway
through production. So did the crew
member operating the onset cooling
system, who killed over from a heart
attack and died immediately. So did a
night watchman who was shot and killed
while guarding the set. Damn, man. Even
Jason Miller's son narrowly avoided
death when he got hit by a motorcycle.
All this death and destruction was
causing two very obvious problems for
the studio. The budget was ballooning
from an optimistic 5 million to well
north of 10. And the shoot, which was
supposed to last just 85 days, ended up
dragging on for more than 200s. To say
the movie was unlucky would be a bit of
an understatement. To say it was [ __ ]
cursed, would probably be more accurate.
In fact, that's exactly what a lot of
the cast and crew were starting to
believe. Like Satan himself was
personally pissed off by this thing and
was trying to stop it from getting
maids. Damn man, I wish he felt this
strongly about Star Wars. He could have
struck down TLJ with great vengeance and
furious anger and I would have been
completely fine with it. The situation
got so bad that Freen had to bring in an
actual Jesuit priest to bless the set
multiple times. Father Thomas Birmingham
and others performed blessings on
several occasions, mostly to calm the
nerves of the crew. But whatever they
did, it seemed to work. Nothing demonic
happened after their work was complete,
apparently. Well, almost. When the movie
was screened in Rome for the first time,
a 400year-old cross on a church opposite
the theater got struck by lightning and
went down in flames.
Boy, that escalated quickly. I mean,
that really got out of hand fast. You
just had to get one more jab in, didn't
you? Either way, The Exorcist was
finally complete and went on wide
release in December 1973. And to say
that it was a hit really doesn't do the
thing justice when audiences weren't
fainting, throwing up, or walking out in
disgust. They were queuing around the
block in freezing winter weather to
experience it for the first time.
Everyone was talking about it, either to
condemn it or praise it. The film went
on to make almost 200 million in its
initial run with another 200 million
later in re-releases, well over a
billion dollars in today's money. Jesus,
even Marvel movies don't have that kind
of pulling power. and it became the
first ever horror movie to be nominated
for an Academy Awards. Not bad stuff,
but was it really cursed? Was Satan
personally trying to stop his own PR
film? Or was it just 1970s Hollywood at
work? Lack safety protocols, an over
ambitious director, freezing sets, dodgy
wiring, and a bird with a death wish.
I'll let you decide. But if you ever
find yourself on a horror set and a
pigeon starts circling the electrics,
get a priest and get the hell out of
there. Because sometimes the real devil
isn't on screen, it's in the details.
Anyway, that's all I've got for today.
Go away now.
